International football competition

The **2002 FIFA World Cup**, also branded as **Korea Japan 2002**, was the 17th [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"), the quadrennial [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") world championship for [men's national](/wiki/List_of_men%27s_national_association_football_teams "List of men's national association football teams") teams organized by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA"). It was held from 31 May to 30 June 2002 at sites in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), with its [final](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_final "2002 FIFA World Cup final") match hosted by Japan at [International Stadium](/wiki/Nissan_Stadium_(Yokohama) "Nissan Stadium (Yokohama)") in [Yokohama](/wiki/Yokohama "Yokohama").

A field of 32 teams qualified for this World Cup, which was the first to be held in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), the first to be held outside of the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") or [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), as well as the first to be jointly hosted by more than one nation. Four teams ([China](/wiki/China_national_football_team "China national football team"), [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador_national_football_team "Ecuador national football team"), [Senegal](/wiki/Senegal_national_football_team "Senegal national football team"), and [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia_national_football_team "Slovenia national football team")) made their World Cup debuts, with Senegal being the only debutant to advance from the Group Stages and make it to the quarter-finals.

The tournament had several upsets and surprise results, which included the defending champions [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") being eliminated in the group stage after earning a single point without scoring a goal, and second favourites [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team") also being eliminated in the group stage. [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_national_football_team "South Korea national football team") managed to reach the semi-finals, beating [Poland](/wiki/Poland_national_football_team "Poland national football team"), [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team") and [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team") en route. They became the first team from outside of the [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA"), [CONMEBOL](/wiki/CONMEBOL "CONMEBOL"), and [CONCACAF](/wiki/CONCACAF "CONCACAF") regions to reach the semi-finals of a World Cup. Along with South Korea, [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team") made its first appearance in the semi-finals as well. However, the most potent team at the tournament, [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team"), prevailed, winning the final against [Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team") 2–0, making them the first and only country to have won the World Cup five times.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In the match for third place against [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_national_football_team "South Korea national football team"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team") won 3–2, taking third place in only their second ever FIFA World Cup, and scored the [fastest goal in the FIFA World Cup history](/wiki/Fastest_goals_in_association_football "Fastest goals in association football") (10.8 seconds after kick-off).[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The 2002 World Cup was also the last one to use the [golden goal](/wiki/Golden_goal "Golden goal") rule and the last one to use the same ball for all matches. Starting in 2006 and continuing to the present, a ball with the same technical specifications but different colors has been used in the final.

## Host selection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Host selection")]

Main article: [FIFA World Cup hosts](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup_hosts "FIFA World Cup hosts")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Boeing_747-4B5%2C_Korean_Air_AN0241562.jpg/250px-Boeing_747-4B5%2C_Korean_Air_AN0241562.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boeing_747-4B5,_Korean_Air_AN0241562.jpg)

[Korean Air](/wiki/Korean_Air "Korean Air") [Boeing 747](/wiki/Boeing_747 "Boeing 747") adorned with 2002 World Cup livery marking South Korea as co-hosts

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/FIFA2002-10000yen.jpg/250px-FIFA2002-10000yen.jpg)](/wiki/File:FIFA2002-10000yen.jpg)

Japanese [10,000 yen coin](/wiki/10,000_yen_coin "10,000 yen coin") for the 2002 FIFA World Cup

South Korea and Japan were selected as hosts by FIFA on 31 May 1996.

Initially, South Korea, Japan and [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") presented three rival bids. South Korea's entry into the race was seen by some as a response to the bid of political and sporting rival Japan.[[3]](#cite_note-Varcoe-3) FIFA leaders were split on whom to favor as host as politics within the world governing body held sway.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) With Mexico regarded as a long shot, the battle to host the tournament came down to South Korea and Japan. The two Asian rivals went on a massive and expensive PR blitz around the world, prompting Sultan [Ahmad Shah](/wiki/Ahmad_Shah_of_Pahang "Ahmad Shah of Pahang"), the head of the [Asian Football Confederation](/wiki/Asian_Football_Confederation "Asian Football Confederation"), to step in.[[3]](#cite_note-Varcoe-3) FIFA boss [João Havelange](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Havelange "João Havelange") had long backed the Japanese bid,[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) but his rival in FIFA, UEFA chief [Lennart Johansson](/wiki/Lennart_Johansson "Lennart Johansson"), sought to undermine Havelange's plans.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") and the AFC viewed co-hosting between the two Asian rivals as the best option.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) South Korea and Japan were finally faced with a choice of having no World Cup or a shared World Cup and they reluctantly chose to go along with co-hosting.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) South Korea and Japan were chosen unanimously as co-hosts in preference to Mexico.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

This was the first World Cup to be hosted by more than one country, the second being the [2026 World Cup](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup"), which will be hosted by the United States, Mexico and Canada. This is also the first ever World Cup to be hosted in Asia, the other being the [2022 World Cup](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup "2022 FIFA World Cup") hosted by [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar") twenty years later and the first World Cup to be held outside of Europe and the Americas. The general secretary of South Korea's bidding committee, Song Young-shik, stated that FIFA was interested in staging some matches in North Korea in order to aid [Korean reunification](/wiki/Korean_reunification "Korean reunification"), but it was ruled out.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Though co-hosting the World Cup allowed Japan and South Korea to collaborate, the event did not significantly alter [relations](/wiki/Japan%E2%80%93South_Korea_relations "Japan–South Korea relations") between the two countries, which have historically been strained.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7) Even so, the World Cup promoted a global vision of cooperation between Japan and South Korea.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7)

After being prevented from hosting along with South Korea, North Korea decided to host their own sports event, the [Arirang Mass Games](/wiki/Arirang_Mass_Games "Arirang Mass Games"), at the same time as the World Cup. This is not unlike their course of action during the 1988 Seoul Olympics, where they decided to host the [13th World Festival of Youth and Students](/wiki/13th_World_Festival_of_Youth_and_Students "13th World Festival of Youth and Students") simultaneously.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

At the time the decision was made, Japan had never qualified for a World Cup final (although the Japanese did subsequently qualify for the [1998 competition](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup")). The only other countries to have been awarded a World Cup without previously having competed in a final tournament are Italy in [1934](/wiki/1934_FIFA_World_Cup "1934 FIFA World Cup") and Qatar in 2022 (Uruguay hosted the first World Cup in [1930](/wiki/1930_FIFA_World_Cup "1930 FIFA World Cup") so there was no prior tournament; they were defending Olympic champions from [1928](/wiki/1928_Summer_Olympics "1928 Summer Olympics")).

## Qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Qualification")]

Main article: [2002 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "2002 FIFA World Cup qualification")

199 teams attempted to qualify for the 2002 World Cup. The qualification process began with the preliminary draw held in Tokyo on 7 December 1999. Defending [champions](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup_Final "1998 FIFA World Cup Final") [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") and co-hosts [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_national_football_team "South Korea national football team") and [Japan](/wiki/Japan_national_football_team "Japan national football team") qualified automatically and did not have to play any qualification matches. This was the final World Cup in which the defending champions qualified automatically.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

14 places were contested by [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") teams (Europe), five by [CAF](/wiki/Confederation_of_African_Football "Confederation of African Football") teams (Africa), four by [CONMEBOL](/wiki/CONMEBOL "CONMEBOL") teams (South America), four by [AFC](/wiki/Asian_Football_Confederation "Asian Football Confederation") teams (Asia) and three by [CONCACAF](/wiki/CONCACAF "CONCACAF") teams (North and Central America and the Caribbean). The remaining two places were decided by playoffs between AFC and UEFA and between CONMEBOL and [OFC](/wiki/Oceania_Football_Confederation "Oceania Football Confederation") (Oceania). Four nations qualified for the finals for the first time: [China](/wiki/China_national_football_team "China national football team"), [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador_national_football_team "Ecuador national football team"), [Senegal](/wiki/Senegal_national_football_team "Senegal national football team") and [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia_national_football_team "Slovenia national football team").

Turkey qualified for the first time since 1954, Poland and Portugal both qualified for the first time since 1986 and Costa Rica and Uruguay qualified for the first time since 1990. Sweden, Russia and the Republic of Ireland also returned after missing the 1998 World Cup. South Korea became the first nation from outside Europe or the Americas to appear five successive finals tournaments.

All seven previous World Cup-winning nations ([Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team"), [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team"), [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team"), and [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_national_football_team "Uruguay national football team")) qualified, which broke the record of most previous champions at a tournament before the record was broken again in 2014.

The lowest ranked team that qualified was China PR (ranked 50th).

## Venues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Venues")]

South Korea and Japan each provided 10 venues, the vast majority of them newly built for the tournament. Groups A–D played all their matches in South Korea and Groups E–H played all their matches in Japan.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The stadiums in Daegu, Suwon, Yokohama and Saitama all hosted 4 matches each, while the other 16 stadiums hosted 3 matches each. Notably, no matches were played in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), making it the second capital of a host country not to have a World Cup venue after [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn") of [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") in [1974](/wiki/1974_FIFA_World_Cup "1974 FIFA World Cup"), however the cities of Saitama and Yokohama which are part of the [Greater Tokyo Area](/wiki/Greater_Tokyo_Area "Greater Tokyo Area") did host matches.[[a]](#cite_note-12)

There was much controversy over the refereeing in the tournament.[[32]](#cite_note-53) Questionable decisions in the match between Italy and South Korea resulted in 400,000 complaints, and featured in ESPN's 10 most fabled World Cup controversies.[[33]](#cite_note-54) The match between Spain and South Korea featured two controversially disallowed Spanish goals, which [Iván Helguera](/wiki/Iv%C3%A1n_Helguera "Iván Helguera") referred to as "a robbery" and led to Spanish press brandishing the officials "thieves of dreams", though FIFA dismissed the incident as human error.[[34]](#cite_note-55)[[35]](#cite_note-56)

## Squads

## Draw

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Draw")]

Further information: [2002 FIFA World Cup seeding](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_seeding "2002 FIFA World Cup seeding")

The FIFA Organising Committee announced the eight seeded teams on 28 November 2001. The historic tradition to seed the hosts (Japan and South Korea) and [holders](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup_Trophy#Winners "FIFA World Cup Trophy") (France) was upheld while the remaining five seeds were granted to the other five of the top six teams—ranked by their results in the last three FIFA World Cups (ratio 3:2:1) and their [FIFA World Ranking](/wiki/FIFA_Men%27s_World_Rankings "FIFA Men's World Rankings") position in the last month of the past three years (equal ratio).[[36]](#cite_note-Seeding-57)

For the draw, the 32 teams were allocated into four pots; the eight top-seeded teams, were allocated in pot 1 and would be drawn/selected into the first position of the eight groups playing in the group stage. The remaining 24 unseeded teams, were allocated into three pots based on geographical sections, with the: 11 European teams in pot 2; two Asian teams and three South American teams in pot 3; three North American teams and five African teams in pot 4.[[37]](#cite_note-DrawProcedure-58)

The general principle was to draw one team from each pot into the eight groups, although with special combined procedures for pot 2 and pot 3, due to comprising more/less than eight teams - but sixteen teams in total. At the same time, the draw also needed to respect the geographical limitation, that each group cannot feature more than one team from each confederation, except for the European teams where the limitation was maximum two per group. Finally, special limitations were also stipulated to evenly distribute the presence of teams from each confederation between the groups playing respectively in Korea (group A-D) and Japan (group E-H); however, for political considerations, China could only be drawn for one of the groups playing in Korea.[[37]](#cite_note-DrawProcedure-58)

* The draw took place at the [Busan Exhibition and Convention Center](/wiki/Busan_Exhibition_and_Convention_Center "Busan Exhibition and Convention Center") in [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") and was televised live on 1 December 2001.

The FIFA Organising Committee decided ahead of the draw to place the defending champions (France) in Group A while the co-hosts South Korea and Japan were placed in Group D and Group H respectively. The procedure for the draw comprised the following six steps:[[37]](#cite_note-DrawProcedure-58)[[38]](#cite_note-Draw_of_Groups-59)

1. Pot 1 was used to draw, in alphabetical group order, the remaining five top-seeded teams for the first position of groups B, C, E, F and G; while respecting the restriction that one of the two South American seeds (Brazil and Argentina) had to play in a group played in South Korea (B/C) and the other had to play in a group played in Japan (E/F/G).
2. Pot 2 was used to draw one [European team](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)") to each of the eight groups (drawing unrestricted in alphabetical order from A to H).
3. As per the FIFA rule of only allowing a maximum of two European teams in each group, the remaining three European teams from Pot 2 were subject to a second draw, to be put in either of the four groups containing a top-seeded [South American team](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(CONMEBOL) "2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONMEBOL)") or [Asian team](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC) "2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)"). This was done by first drawing the European team from Pot 2, and then drawing which seeded opponent the European team should be paired with, from a special bowl with four blue balls containing the names of Brazil, Argentina, Japan and South Korea.
4. Pot 3 was used to draw one team to each of the five groups with an empty third slot (drawing in alphabetical order from A to H); while respecting the geographical restrictions, that:
   1. None of the unseeded South American teams (Ecuador, Paraguay, Uruguay) from pot 3 could be drawn into a group with a seeded South American team (Brazil and Argentina).
   2. None of the unseeded Asian teams (Saudi Arabia and China) from pot 3 could be drawn into a group with a seeded Asian team (South Korea and Japan in Group D and H); along with the overall rule that China had to play in South Korea (meaning either group A, B or C) and that Saudi Arabia had to play in Japan (meaning either group E, F or G).
5. Pot 4 was used to draw one team to each of the eight groups (drawing in alphabetical order from A to H); while respecting the restrictions that:
   1. A minimum of one [North American team](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(CONCACAF) "2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (CONCACAF)") and minimum of two [African teams](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(CAF) "2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (CAF)") should be drawn to a group located in South Korea (Group A/B/C/D)
   2. A minimum of one North American team and minimum of two African teams should be drawn to a group located in Japan (Group E/F/G/H)
6. To decide the match schedules, the exact group position number for the un-seeded teams in each group (2, 3 or 4), were also drawn immediately from eight special group bowls, after each respective team had been drawn from pot 2, 3 and 4.

Besides drawing the teams, the event also featured American vocalist [Anastacia](/wiki/Anastacia "Anastacia") giving a debut public performance of the [official song of the World Cup](/wiki/List_of_FIFA_World_Cup_anthems_and_songs "List of FIFA World Cup anthems and songs"), "[Boom](/wiki/Boom_(Anastacia_song) "Boom (Anastacia song)")".[[39]](#cite_note-draw_history-60)[[40]](#cite_note-Boom-61) The draw was conducted by FIFA general secretary [Michel Zen-Ruffinen](/wiki/Michel_Zen-Ruffinen "Michel Zen-Ruffinen"), and many celebrities helped with the draw such as former players [Pelé](/wiki/Pel%C3%A9 "Pelé"), [Johan Cruyff](/wiki/Johan_Cruyff "Johan Cruyff"), [Michel Platini](/wiki/Michel_Platini "Michel Platini"), [Enrique Borja](/wiki/Enrique_Borja "Enrique Borja") and [Roger Milla](/wiki/Roger_Milla "Roger Milla"), players of the time like [Hong Myung-bo](/wiki/Hong_Myung-bo "Hong Myung-bo") and [Masami Ihara](/wiki/Masami_Ihara "Masami Ihara"), women's football player [Sun Wen](/wiki/Sun_Wen_(footballer) "Sun Wen (footballer)"), women's football referee [Im Eun-ju](/wiki/Im_Eun-ju "Im Eun-ju"), mountain climber [Park Young-seok](/wiki/Park_Young-seok "Park Young-seok"), actress [Song Hye-kyo](/wiki/Song_Hye-kyo "Song Hye-kyo") and chess player [Cho Hoon-hyun](/wiki/Cho_Hoon-hyun "Cho Hoon-hyun").

In each group, the teams played three matches, one against each of the other teams. Victories were granted 3 points, while a draw was equal to 1 point. After completion of the group stage, the best two teams of each group advanced to the Round of 16 in the [knockout stage](#Knockout_stage), in a way so all group winners started out meeting a runner-up from one of the other groups. This format was identical with the tournament structure being used in [1998](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup"). A total of 64 games were played, including the [final](#Final) and a [match for third place](#Match_for_third_place) between the two semi-final losers.

Group F was considered the [group of death](/wiki/Group_of_death "Group of death") as it brought together Argentina, England, Nigeria and Sweden.[[39]](#cite_note-draw_history-60)

In the following tables:

* **Pld** = total games played
* **W** = total games won
* **D** = total games drawn (tied)
* **L** = total games lost
* **GF** = total goals scored (goals for)
* **GA** = total goals conceded (goals against)
* **GD** = goal difference (GF−GA)
* **Pts** = total points accumulated

The teams in the group play were ranked upon

* Points
* Greatest total goal difference in the three group matches
* Greatest number of goals scored in the three group matches
* Most points earned in matches against other teams in the tie
* Greatest goal difference in matches against other teams in the tie
* Greatest number of goals scored in matches against other teams in the tie
* Drawing of lots

In the original version of the rules for the final tournament, the ranking criteria were in a different order, with head-to-head results taking precedence over total goal difference. The rules were changed to the above in advance of the tournament, but older versions were still available on the FIFA and UEFA websites, causing some confusion among those trying to identify the correct criteria.[[41]](#cite_note-62)

### Round of 16

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Round of 16")]

In the round of 16, Germany beat Paraguay 1–0 with a late goal by [Oliver Neuville](/wiki/Oliver_Neuville "Oliver Neuville") in Seogwipo. England defeated Denmark in Niigata 3–0, with all goals occurring in the first half of the game. Sweden and Senegal faced off in Ōita and finished 1–1 in regular time and it took a [golden goal](/wiki/Golden_goal "Golden goal") from [Henri Camara](/wiki/Henri_Camara "Henri Camara") in extra time to settle the game for Senegal 2–1, which led to Senegal becoming only the second African team to reach the last eight (after [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon_national_football_team "Cameroon national football team") in [1990](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup "1990 FIFA World Cup")). Spain and the Republic of Ireland played in Suwon, where Spain led most of the match 1–0 until a late penalty kick scored by Robbie Keane made the match go to extra time, where Spain emerged victorious in a penalty shoot-out. The United States beat CONCACAF rivals Mexico 2–0 in Jeonju with [Brian McBride](/wiki/Brian_McBride "Brian McBride") and [Landon Donovan](/wiki/Landon_Donovan "Landon Donovan") scoring the goals. Brazil defeated Belgium 2–0 in Kobe, with an amazing volley by [Rivaldo](/wiki/Rivaldo "Rivaldo") and a splendid counter-attack goal by [Ronaldo](/wiki/Ronaldo_(Brazilian_footballer) "Ronaldo (Brazilian footballer)"). Turkey ended co-hosts Japan's run with a 1–0 win in Miyagi, thanks to an [Ümit Davala](/wiki/%C3%9Cmit_Davala "Ümit Davala") goal in the 12th minute. The other co-hosts, South Korea, defeated Italy 2–1 in extra time in Daejeon with a goal by [Ahn Jung-hwan](/wiki/Ahn_Jung-hwan "Ahn Jung-hwan") in the 117th minute.[[42]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-63) South Korea's win ensured that, for the very first time in the Cup's history, teams from five continents – Europe, North America, South America, Africa and Asia – reached the quarter-finals of the same tournament.

### Quarter-finals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Quarter-finals")]

In the quarter-finals, England and Brazil squared off in Shizuoka, where [Ronaldinho](/wiki/Ronaldinho "Ronaldinho") scored a free-kick goal over England's [David Seaman](/wiki/David_Seaman "David Seaman") early in the second half as Brazil won 2–1.[[43]](#cite_note-64) The United States lost to Germany 1–0 in Ulsan by a [Michael Ballack](/wiki/Michael_Ballack "Michael Ballack") goal in the 39th minute, but controversy surrounded the game when United States demanded the referee give a penalty for a goal-line handball by [Torsten Frings](/wiki/Torsten_Frings "Torsten Frings") in the 49th minute, but the referee did not award the penalty. South Korea got another success in [Gwangju](/wiki/Gwangju "Gwangju") in a controversial manner, overcoming Spain 5–3 on penalties after a 0–0 draw in which the Spaniards twice thought they had scored while onside; however, the efforts were disallowed by the referee with controversial decisions.[[44]](#cite_note-goal.com-65)[[45]](#cite_note-auto-66) The hosts became the first team in the [Asian Football Confederation](/wiki/Asian_Football_Confederation "Asian Football Confederation") to reach the semi-finals of the World Cup, eclipsing the record of their [North Korean counterparts](/wiki/North_Korea_national_football_team "North Korea national football team") who reached the quarter-finals in [1966](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup"). They also became the first World Cup semi-final team not from UEFA or CONMEBOL since the United States did it in the first World Cup in 1930. Turkey defeated Senegal 1–0 in Osaka, with a golden goal scored by [İlhan Mansız](/wiki/%C4%B0lhan_Mans%C4%B1z "İlhan Mansız") in the 94th minute.

### Semi-finals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Semi-finals")]

The semi-finals saw two 1–0 games; the first semi-final, played in Seoul, saw Michael Ballack's goal suffice for Germany to eliminate South Korea. However, Ballack had already received a yellow card during the match before, which forced him to miss the final based on accumulated yellow cards.[[46]](#cite_note-67) The next day in Saitama saw Ronaldo score a goal early in the second half, his sixth of the competition for Brazil, to defeat Turkey in a replay of their Group C encounter.[[47]](#cite_note-68)[[48]](#cite_note-69)

### Match for third place

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Match for third place")]

In the match for third place in [Daegu](/wiki/Daegu "Daegu"), Turkey beat the South Koreans 3–2, their first goal coming from [Hakan Şükür](/wiki/Hakan_%C5%9E%C3%BCk%C3%BCr "Hakan Şükür") straight from the opening kick-off (even though South Korea kicked off) in 10.8 seconds, the fastest ever goal in World Cup history.[[49]](#cite_note-70)

### Final

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Final")]

Main article: [2002 FIFA World Cup final](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_final "2002 FIFA World Cup final")

In the [final match](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_final "2002 FIFA World Cup final") held in Yokohama, Japan, two goals from Ronaldo secured the World Cup for [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team") as they claimed victory over [Germany](/wiki/German_national_football_team "German national football team").[[50]](#cite_note-71) Ronaldo scored twice in the second half and, after the game, won the [Golden Shoe](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup_awards "FIFA World Cup awards") award for the tournament's leading scorer with eight goals.[[51]](#cite_note-72) This was the fifth time Brazil had won the World Cup, cementing their status as the most successful national team in the history of the competition. Brazil became the only team since Argentina in [1986](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup "1986 FIFA World Cup") to win the trophy without needing to win a [penalty shoot-out](/wiki/Penalty_shoot-out_(association_football) "Penalty shoot-out (association football)") at some stage during the knockout phase and the total number of penalty shoot-outs (2) was the lowest since the four-round knockout format was introduced in 1986. Brazil also became the first team to win every match at a World Cup since 1970 and set a new record for highest aggregate goal difference (+14) for a World Cup winner. Brazil's captain [Cafu](/wiki/Cafu "Cafu"), who became the first player to appear in three successive World Cup finals, accepted the trophy on behalf of the team.

### Ticket sales problem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Ticket sales problem")]

The original domestic ticket allocation had fully sold out and the organising committee completed sales of tickets returned from the international allocation by the end of April. However, there were a significant number of empty seats at the opening matches.[[74]](#cite_note-95) It was gradually revealed that the World Cup Ticketing Bureau (WCTB) still had unsold tickets in its possession. After FIFA agreed to sell this inventory, JAWOC undertook sales over telephone and WCTB handled the internet sales.[[75]](#cite_note-96) For the second round Japan vs. Turkey match in Miyagi in particular, although it was reported by both parties that all tickets had been sold, some 700 seats remained empty.

## Symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Symbols")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/53/Koreajapan2002mascots.png/250px-Koreajapan2002mascots.png)](/wiki/File:Koreajapan2002mascots.png)

Ato, Kaz and Nik were the 2002 World Cup mascots.

### Mascot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Mascot")]

The [official mascots](/wiki/List_of_FIFA_World_Cup_official_mascots "List of FIFA World Cup official mascots") of the 2002 World Cup were Ato, Kaz and Nik (the Spheriks), orange, purple and blue (respectively) futuristic [CGI](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") creatures. Playing their own version of soccer called Atmoball, Ato is the coach while Kaz and Nik are players. The three individual names were selected from shortlists by users on the Internet and at [McDonald's](/wiki/McDonald%27s "McDonald's") outlets in the host countries.[[76]](#cite_note-97)

### Match ball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Match ball")]

Main article: [Adidas Fevernova](/wiki/Adidas_Fevernova "Adidas Fevernova")

The [official match ball](/wiki/List_of_FIFA_World_Cup_official_match_balls "List of FIFA World Cup official match balls") was the [Fevernova](/wiki/Adidas_Fevernova "Adidas Fevernova"), manufactured by [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas").[[77]](#cite_note-98)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Music")]

The [official song](/wiki/List_of_FIFA_World_Cup_songs_and_anthems "List of FIFA World Cup songs and anthems") was "[Boom](/wiki/Boom_(Anastacia_song) "Boom (Anastacia song)")".[[78]](#cite_note-99) The official local song of this World Cup was "[Let's Get Together Now](/wiki/Let%27s_Get_Together_Now "Let's Get Together Now")". The official anthem was "[Anthem](/wiki/Anthem_(FIFA_World_Cup) "Anthem (FIFA World Cup)")".

## Cultural event

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Cultural event")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/%22In_Search_of_Fresh_Air%22%2C_by_Ray_L._Burggraf.jpg/250px-%22In_Search_of_Fresh_Air%22%2C_by_Ray_L._Burggraf.jpg)](/wiki/File:%22In_Search_of_Fresh_Air%22,_by_Ray_L._Burggraf.jpg)

*In Search of Fresh Air*, banner by Ray L. Burggraf

The official FIFA cultural event of the 2002 World Cup was a flag festival called *Poetry of the Winds*.[[79]](#cite_note-le-musee-divisioniste2002-100) Held in [Nanjicheon Park](/wiki/World_Cup_Park#Nanjicheon_Park "World Cup Park"), an area of the World Cup Park close to Seoul World Cup Stadium,[[80]](#cite_note-101)[[81]](#cite_note-102) *Poetry of the Winds* was exhibited from 29 May to 25 June in order to wish success upon the World Cup and promote a festive atmosphere. During the flag art festival, hand-painted flags from global artists were displayed as a greeting to international guests in a manner that was designed to promote harmony.[[79]](#cite_note-le-musee-divisioniste2002-100)

## Concerns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Concerns")]

The World Cup was originally going to be hosted either in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") or in [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), but in the end both rivals had decided to share the hosting duties thus making this World Cup the first to have multiple host nations. However, there were concerns regarding the selection of hosts due to logistical issues caused by fans traveling across two separate sovereign nations as well as whether some of the 20 stadiums to be constructed for the World Cup would be ready in time for it or not. While political and infrastructural problems were eventually overcome, there still remained the issue of East Asia's wet season which could disrupt the play. The timing of the tournament thus had been altered to mitigate as much as possible against such issues, with the tournament kicking off on May 31 and due to run until June 30, the earliest date for a World Cup final since 1986.[[82]](#cite_note-103)

The time difference caused issues for fans worldwide especially in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), where people had to go to work when matches were played and were used to games taking place at other times.[[83]](#cite_note-104)[[84]](#cite_note-Sports_Marketing:_Beer_for_Breakfast-105) With games taking place in the European morning, some schools and businesses chose to open late on match days or set up communal watching events before the start of work.[[85]](#cite_note-School_succumbs_to_football_fever-106)[[86]](#cite_note-World_Cup_2002:_a_shot_at_goal-107)

## Aftermath and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2002_FIFA_World_Cup&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Aftermath and legacy")]

The tournament had a major economic impact on both South Korea and Japan, generating an estimated US$1.3 billion in revenue.[[87]](#cite_note-LeeTaylor-108) Spending from World Cup tourists in South Korea created US$307 million in direct income and US$713 million in valued added.[[87]](#cite_note-LeeTaylor-108) Japan spent an estimated US$5.6 billion on preparations for the event, which had a US$24.8 billion impact on the Japanese economy and accounted for 0.6% of their GDP in 2002.[[88]](#cite_note-109)
